lines.text: Welcome back to PRGE, folks. Quinn Foley here, and we are {{ELAPSED}} into the evening, and I gotta say — it's quiet.
Not good quiet. Not 'everybody stayed home' quiet. Bad quiet. The kind of quiet that makes your teeth itch.
Tim's been feeding me the early numbers and they're lighter than last year at this point — which should feel like a win, right?
Except it doesn't. It feels like someone forgot to show up to their own party.
Or like they're waiting. And I don't like waiting.
You know who liked waiting? Senator Briggs. Remember Briggs? No, you don't, because he's been dead for eight years, but he used to say that patience was a virtue.
Briggs said a lot of things. Most of them were lies. All of them were delivered in a suit that cost more than your car.
Pours a whiskey here — first one's on me, folks, medicinal purposes — and I'm thinking about what Briggs would say if he could see Madison tonight.
Actually, scratch that. Briggs would have paid to get out of Madison before the Purge started. Briggs got out of everything that mattered.
We've got a caller on the line. Greg from East Side, you're on with Quinn Foley. What's happening out there?
Caller: Greg from East Side — Man, it's too quiet up here. I got my panels up, doors barricaded, but I'm not hearing anything. Usually by now there's sirens.
No sirens tonight, Greg. That's actually accurate. What's your street look like right now?
Caller: Greg from East Side — Dark. Empty. I saw one car drive past real slow about twenty minutes ago. Didn't stop.
That car was looking. The fact that it didn't stop might actually be worse than if it had, Greg. You got neighbors?
Caller: Greg from East Side — Got the Hendersons to my left. They didn't install anything. I can see their lights are off but like... they didn't leave either?
They're in the dark by choice, Greg. That's a very specific kind of terror. You stay locked in and don't go checking on anybody, understood?
Caller: Greg from East Side — Understood. Thanks for being here. This helps.
You got it, Greg. Stay with us. We'll be here until 7am.
That's the thing about the quiet — it makes you want to do something stupid. It makes you want to check on people. It makes you feel like a coward for staying home.
But staying home is the only smart move. Staying home is the revolution. And I say that as a man who hasn't made a smart move since 2165.
That was the year I made the mistake of believing that the people I worked with actually cared about the people they worked for. What a concept.
I was in Regional Governance. Sounds important, right? It wasn't. It was meetings and handshakes and knowing when to look the other way.
I got very good at the last part. Too good. Became a liability when it turned out that looking the other way has a limit.
So now I'm here, {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge, drinking whiskey on a pirate frequency that the government could shut down any minute if they felt like it.
Which they won't. Because I don't exist on any other night of the year, so I'm not worth the resources to hunt down in the legal hours.
That's the deal. The deal is, I get to broadcast one night a year, and the deal is, nobody pretends I'm not a ghost the rest of the time.
We've got another call coming in. Sarah from Willy Street — Sarah, you're on with Quinn.
Caller: Sarah from Willy Street — I've been listening since 19:00 and I just wanted to say that your voice is the only thing keeping me from — from really falling apart right now.
Sarah, I appreciate that, but I'm going to be honest with you — I'm not the person to keep you from falling apart. I'm the person who fell apart.
Caller: Sarah from Willy Street — Yeah, but you're still here. And you're still talking. That counts for something.
It counts for one night a year, Sarah. Tonight. Stay on the line if you want to. We can talk about nothing until the sun comes up.
Caller: Sarah from Willy Street — Okay. Okay, I'll stay.
That's what I'm here for. That's what this whole thing is for. You're not supposed to be alone on Purge Night, even if you're locked in your house.
Even if you're trapped. Even if you're scared. You've got a voice on the radio telling you that somebody else is awake, and somebody else knows you're scared.
Tim's just handed me an update — kill count for the hour is lower than projected, but the neighborhoods that are reporting are concentrated.
East Side's still running hot. Capitol Square had an incident around 20:40. Three confirmed. And State Street is... Tim says State Street is showing coordinated activity.
Coordinated is worse than random. Random you can hide from. Coordinated means somebody's got a plan. Coordinated means somebody's got a list.
But we don't talk about that on air. We talk about the weather. We talk about the sky. We talk about literally anything else.
The irony is, you can't talk about the Purge on any other night. You can't acknowledge it exists. You can't say the word in a government building.
But you can broadcast it. You can talk about it. You can sit here and I can talk to you about how quiet is scarier than noise.
Because it's legal. Because it's the Purge. Because for one night, the rules are different and the people in charge have to stay locked in their offices too.
Director Calloway is sitting in a Level-10 bunker right now. I know Calloway. I had lunch with the man.
He always ordered the same thing — a salad, no dressing, water, black coffee. He was very concerned with what people thought of his diet.
What I remember more is the time he casually mentioned that the Purge wasn't designed to stop crime, it was designed to redistribute it.
He said that like it was obvious. Like I should have already understood. Like anyone with a brain would know that the whole point is to let the pressure out in a controlled way.
And the trick is, the way you control it is by making sure that the pressure doesn't blow up at the people who are already sitting on top.
So the East Side gets hot. State Street runs coordinated. The neighborhoods that can't afford security see their numbers spike. And Calloway sits in a bunker and eats a salad.
That's not a conspiracy theory, folks. That's just the Purge working exactly as intended.
Pours another whiskey. Two down, might be seven to go by the time the sun comes up.
We've got a text-in from someone calling themselves 'West Side Watcher.' They're saying activity is starting to move west from Capitol Square.
West Side Watcher, if you're still listening — we can't repeat unconfirmed reports, but we can hear what you're saying, and we appreciate you being our eyes out there.
Stay locked up. Don't engage. Just keep us posted if you feel safe enough to do that.
The reason we take reports from the street is because Tucker can only be in one place, and Madison's a lot of places.
Tucker's good at what they do — they're out there right now, probably in a hot zone, probably in the kind of danger that most people wouldn't sign up for even once, let alone every year.
And they come back. Not every year intact, but they come back. That's the kind of person Tucker is.
The rest of us — we've got the luxury of staying home. We've got the luxury of being scared behind closed doors.
And we're supposed to feel grateful for that. And mostly, I do.
Mostly, I'm grateful that I get to sit here and talk to people like Sarah from Willy Street instead of being out there where it's hot.
But I'm also thinking about the people who don't get to stay home. The people who don't have a choice because the Purge doesn't give them one.
Another caller. Michelle from Park Street — Michelle, you're on with Quinn.
Caller: Michelle from Park Street — I just wanted to check in. I haven't heard anything in my neighborhood for about forty minutes and I'm starting to worry that that's a bad thing.
Silence is always a bad thing, Michelle. But silence in Park Street usually means people are home where they should be, which is exactly what you want.
Caller: Michelle from Park Street — That's what I keep telling myself. But my daughter won't stop asking if it's over yet. She's nine.
Okay. Tell your daughter that there's a man on the radio who's going to stay on the radio until seven in the morning, and she can listen to him if she gets scared.
Caller: Michelle from Park Street — Can I tell her your name?
Tell her it's Quinn. Just Quinn. And tell her that she's going to be fine because her mom's taking care of her, and that's the most important thing.
Caller: Michelle from Park Street — Thank you. I'm going to stay on all night if that's okay.
That's exactly what I'm here for, Michelle. You stay on. Your daughter can sleep. You keep the volume down and you just know that we're here.
That's three regulars now — Greg, Sarah, Michelle — and the night's still young. The night is still very young.
This is what I think about when people ask me why I do this. Why I come back every year. Why I risk everything one night a year to sit in a radio station and talk to people I'll never meet.
It's because Michelle's daughter is nine years old and she's scared and the only way I can help is to be a voice that says 'you're not alone.'
It's because the system that creates the Purge counts on you feeling isolated. It counts on you believing that everyone else is out there taking part.
But they're not. Most people are home. Most people are terrified. Most people are exactly where Michelle is right now.
And if I can be the voice that tells them that's okay — that hiding is not cowardice, that staying home is the only ethical move — then I'm worth the risk.
Even if I'm a ghost the other three hundred and sixty-four days of the year.
Even if my career is a burning building behind me. Even if everyone I worked with has moved on and pretends I never existed.
I exist tonight. And that's enough.
Third whiskey going down. The night's starting to soften around the edges for me, which is either a good sign or the beginning of a very bad decision.
Tim's indicating that we're {{ELAPSED}} in and the kill count is still running low for this point in the night, but concentrated.
That means whoever's doing the work is doing it efficiently. That means they know what they're doing.
But we don't talk about that. We don't speculate. We just put out the numbers and let people draw their own conclusions.
That's the deal with PRGE. We report. We don't editorialize. Well, I editorialize. But that's because I'm drunk and sad and I can get away with it.
One more call coming in. James from North Side — James, what's happening in your part of town?
Caller: James from North Side — It's been clean so far. Too clean, maybe. I'm on Sherman and I haven't heard a single siren all night.
The North Side was quiet last year too. That's actually your advantage. Sirens don't mean anything good on Purge Night, James.
Caller: James from North Side — That's what I figured. I just wanted to know I wasn't going crazy.
You're not going crazy. You're exactly sane. Sane enough to know that quiet might actually be a good sign.
That's it for now, folks. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and we've got a long night ahead of us. Stay with us. Stay home. Stay alive.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide: 38 confirmed (cumulative). East Side +5 this hour. Capitol Square: 3 confirmed 20:40. State Street showing coordinated activity — count pending.
West Side Watcher reports movement from Capitol Square heading west. Unconfirmed. Residents advised to remain locked down.
Multiple callers confirming unusually quiet conditions in residential zones. Pattern consistent with year-over-year dip. Analysts uncertain if temporary or sustained.
PRGE broadcasts until 07:00 Madison time. All listeners advised: remain indoors, maintain locked entry points, keep volume low on broadcast.
